skill.name: charset-fix
skill.description: Fix Chinese/Unicode character encoding issues when running AI agents on Windows via POSIX shells (Git Bash, MSYS2, WSL, BusyBox, etc.). Handles Python, PowerShell, and cmd.exe GBK/CP936 output encoding mismatch with UTF-8 terminals.
skill.summary: Fix garbled Chinese character output from AI agents on Windows POSIX terminals by applying the correct encoding workaround for Python, PowerShell, and cmd.exe.
skill.author: gkd2323c

security_audit.summary: All 78 static findings are false positives. The 71 external_commands detections are code examples in documentation (README.md and SKILL.md) showing CLI commands for fixing encoding issues, plus a legitimate diagnostic helper script (scripts/fix.py) using subprocess.run with hardcoded arguments. The 3 network detections are GitHub badge URLs in markdown, not executed code. The high-entropy and weak-crypto alerts are false positives caused by Chinese text content and encoding-related keywords. No confirmed security threats identified.

low_findings.title: Documentation contains command-line examples with PowerShell and cmd.exe
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE - README.md and SKILL.md contain shell command examples showing PowerShell, cmd.exe, and Python subprocess commands. These are documentation code blocks for user reference, not executed code. The commands demonstrate encoding fixes for Windows users.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Code blocks in markdown documentation are static text, not executable. The content explains encoding workarounds for Windows POSIX terminal users.

low_findings.title: Diagnostic script uses subprocess.run for encoding checks
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE - scripts/fix.py calls subprocess.run with hardcoded commands ('powershell.exe chcp' and 'cmd.exe /c ver') for legitimate encoding diagnostics. Arguments are literal strings, no user input injection point exists. This is standard diagnostic tooling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All subprocess.run calls use hardcoded string arguments. No dynamic input, no shell=True, no injection vector. Commands are standard Windows diagnostic tools.

low_findings.title: GitHub badge URLs detected as network indicators
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE - README.md contains Markdown image badge URLs (img.shields.io) for license and skill badges. These are static documentation references, not programmatic network calls made by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Shields.io badge URLs in Markdown are a universal documentation convention. The skill has no code that makes network requests.

low_findings.title: False positive - weak cryptographic algorithm detection
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE - Static analyzer flagged 'weak cryptographic algorithm' at SKILL.md lines 3, 100, and 102. These are YAML frontmatter (line 3: description field) and table cells explaining encoding variables (line 100: PYTHONIOENCODING=utf-8, line 102: subprocess encoding='gbk'). No cryptographic operations exist in this skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text references encoding-related environment variables and parameters, not cryptographic algorithms. The static analyzer likely confused encoding with encryption terminology.

low_findings.title: High file entropy from Chinese text content
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE - README.md shows high entropy (6.27 bits) due to CJK (Chinese) characters mixed with ASCII text. Chinese characters use multi-byte UTF-8 encoding which increases entropy. This is a known false positive in text files with non-ASCII scripts, not obfuscation or encrypted content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: README.md contains extensive Chinese text explaining the encoding fix. The high entropy is an artifact of CJK characters, which occupy 3 bytes each in UTF-8.

content.user_title: Fix Garbled Chinese Text on Windows AI Terminals
content.value_statement: AI agents on Windows POSIX shells (Git Bash, MSYS2, WSL) often display garbled Chinese text due to GBK and UTF-8 encoding mismatches. This skill detects the mismatch and applies the correct fix for Python, PowerShell, and cmd.exe output encoding.
actual_capabilities: Sets PYTHONIOENCODING=utf-8 to fix garbled Python output in POSIX terminals
Configures PowerShell console output encoding to UTF-8
Provides a GBK-aware subprocess bridge for cmd.exe native commands
Verifies encoding fix with built-in diagnostic test commands
Works across Git Bash, MSYS2, BusyBox, and WSL on Windows
limitations: Not needed on macOS or Linux systems where UTF-8 is the default
Does not fix file content encoding, only terminal output encoding
PowerShell Core (pwsh.exe) already uses UTF-8 and does not need this fix
Requires Python 3 to be installed on the Windows system

use_cases.title: Claude Code on Windows with Git Bash
use_cases.description: When running Claude Code or Codex CLI on Windows through Git Bash, Python output containing Chinese characters appears as garbled text. This skill detects the encoding mismatch and applies the correct environment variable settings.
use_cases.target_user: AI agent users on Chinese Windows systems

use_cases.title: Cross-platform development workflow
use_cases.description: A developer using Windows with a POSIX shell environment for AI-assisted coding encounters garbled text from Python scripts. The skill ensures all terminal output is properly encoded without manual configuration.
use_cases.target_user: Developers working across Windows and POSIX environments

use_cases.title: Migrating from cmd.exe to modern shells
use_cases.description: Users switching from Windows cmd.exe to Git Bash or WSL find that their Chinese text output breaks. This skill explains the encoding root cause and provides automated fixes.
use_cases.target_user: Windows users transitioning to POSIX-compatible shells

prompt_templates.title: Fix garbled Python output
prompt_templates.prompt: My Python scripts output garbled Chinese characters when run through Git Bash. Apply the charset-fix encoding workaround.
prompt_templates.scenario: User sees garbled text from Python on Windows POSIX terminal

prompt_templates.title: Check current encoding environment
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the encoding check to see the current system code page, Python encoding, and PowerShell encoding settings.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to diagnose their current encoding state

prompt_templates.title: Test all encoding fixes
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a full encoding test suite for Python, PowerShell, and cmd.exe to verify all encoding fixes are working correctly.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to verify the fix is applied across all tools

prompt_templates.title: Create a persistent encoding fix script
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a startup script that sets PYTHONIOENCODING=utf-8 and configures PowerShell UTF-8 output automatically when I open Git Bash.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants a permanent encoding fix for their development environment

output_examples.input: Run python3 -c 'print("中文测试")' in Git Bash on Windows without charset-fix
output_examples.output:
Before: ���Ĳ���
After: 中文测试

The fix adds PYTHONIOENCODING=utf-8 to the Python environment, converting garbled output to readable Chinese characters.

output_examples.input: Run the encoding diagnostic check on a Windows system
output_examples.output:
System code page: 936
Python stdout encoding: utf-8
echo 中文测试: 中文测试 (normal)

This output confirms the system uses GBK code page 936 and Python stdout has been corrected to UTF-8.

best_practices: Set PYTHONIOENCODING=utf-8 as an environment variable for your shell profile so all Python sessions use UTF-8 output automatically.
Use pwsh.exe (PowerShell Core) instead of powershell.exe when possible, as it uses UTF-8 natively and does not need encoding workarounds.
Verify encoding fixes with a simple test command before running complex scripts to catch encoding issues early.
anti_patterns: Running chcp 65001 before every command to change code page, which is unreliable and breaks some Windows tools.
Modifying system-wide locale settings or registry values without understanding the consequences for other applications.
Assuming the same encoding fix applies to all shells on Windows without testing each environment separately.

faq.question: What causes garbled Chinese text in AI agents on Windows?
faq.answer: Windows native tools (Python, PowerShell, cmd.exe) on Chinese systems default to GBK or CP936 encoding. POSIX shells like Git Bash and WSL expect UTF-8. The mismatch produces garbled output.

faq.question: Does this skill work on macOS or Linux?
faq.answer: No. macOS and Linux already use UTF-8 by default and do not have this encoding mismatch problem. This skill is only needed on Windows.

faq.question: What does PYTHONIOENCODING do?
faq.answer: It overrides Python's automatic stdout encoding detection. Setting it to utf-8 forces Python to output UTF-8 text regardless of the system code page.

faq.question: Does PowerShell Core (pwsh.exe) need this fix?
faq.answer: No. PowerShell Core uses UTF-8 by default. Only the legacy Windows PowerShell (powershell.exe) has the GBK encoding problem.

faq.question: How do I install this skill for Claude Code?
faq.answer: Clone the repository and run 'claude add-skill ./' from the charset-fix directory. See the README for alternative installation methods for Codex CLI, Cline, and Cursor.

faq.question: Will this skill fix file encoding issues?
faq.answer: No. This skill only addresses terminal output encoding. File encoding issues in source code or data files require separate tools like iconv or file conversion utilities.
